report_name: 'Trapdoor' Android Ad Fraud Campaign Used 455 Malicious Apps to Hijack Millions of Devices

attack_patterns.name: Download New Code at Runtime
attack_patterns.description: The Trapdoor campaign used a two-stage approach where initial 'dropper' apps downloaded from the Google Play Store would then trick users into installing a secondary, malicious payload app.

attack_patterns.name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
attack_patterns.description: The Trapdoor malware would only activate its malicious payload if it determined the app was installed via a paid malvertising campaign, checking the device's mobile marketing attribution data. This was used to evade detection from researchers who might install the app organically.

attack_patterns.name: Generate Traffic from Victim
attack_patterns.description: The core function of the Trapdoor malware was to generate fraudulent ad revenue by opening hidden browser windows to load ads and programmatically click on them, creating up to 659 million fraudulent bid requests in a single day.

attack_patterns.name: Hide Artifacts
attack_patterns.description: The Stage 2 payload app ran in the background, using hidden WebView components to load attacker-controlled web pages and simulate ad clicks without the user's knowledge or visibility.

malware.name: Trapdoor
malware.description: A family of 455 malicious Android applications involved in a large-scale ad fraud operation. The malware uses a two-stage infection, with a dropper app leading to a payload app that generates fraudulent ad clicks in hidden background WebViews.

campaigns.name: Trapdoor
campaigns.description: A large-scale, sophisticated mobile ad fraud campaign that used a network of 455 malicious Android apps on the Google Play Store to generate millions of dollars in fraudulent ad revenue.

identities.name: Advertisers and Ad Networks
identities.description: The primary financial victims of the Trapdoor ad fraud campaign, who paid for fraudulent ad impressions and clicks generated by the malware.

identities.name: Google
identities.description: Operator of the Google Play Store, from which the 455 malicious apps associated with the Trapdoor campaign were removed.

infrastructure.name: Trapdoor Ad Fraud Web Pages
infrastructure.description: Attacker-controlled web pages loaded into hidden WebViews on compromised devices. These pages were filled with ads that the malware would programmatically click to generate fraudulent revenue.

indicators.name: Anomalous Background Ad Network Traffic from WebView
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Background Ad Network Traffic from WebView
id: 2b9c1f8a-1e3d-4b7c-8a2d-9f6e3c5a7b1d
status: experimental
description: >
  Detects network traffic to common ad network domains originating from a generic Android WebView user-agent, not a standard browser. When observed at high frequency, this could indicate background ad fraud activity like the Trapdoor campaign.
references:
  - https://www.cybersecuritynews.com/trapdoor-android-ad-fraud/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-24
tags:
  - attack.t1643
level: low
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    cs-host|endswith:
      - '.doubleclick.net'
      - '.googleadservices.com'
      - '.googlesyndication.com'
      - '.admob.com'
      - '.adsrvr.org'
    c-useragent|contains: 'WebView'
  filter_legit_browsers:
    c-useragent|contains:
      - 'Chrome/'
      - 'Firefox/'
      - 'Edge/'
  condition: selection and not filter_legit_browsers
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate applications that display ads using an in-app WebView.
indicators.description: Detects network traffic to common ad network domains originating from a generic Android WebView user-agent. High volumes of such traffic could indicate ad fraud activity similar to the Trapdoor campaign, which used hidden WebViews to click on ads.

relationships.source_name: Anomalous Background Ad Network Traffic from WebView
